speaker: interviewer
text: When did you part with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: I part with my sister in in the-- in the middle of December of 1929.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, when did-- after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, after the war-- in January of '53, because she remained in Montreal, and I came to St. Louis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it hard for you to keep her Jewish faith?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, then she-- no, she knew all the time that she's Jewish. But she had to survive. So you know she did the best she could. After all, she was only nine years old when the war broke out. And she was on her own most of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was hard. And we parted. I came to this country in the 20th of January of 1953. And my sister married and remained in Montreal, with her husband. And she lived as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, she converted to Judaism. She is quite religious. She keeps a kosher home and keeps all the high holidays. Observes very, very highly, more than I do, all the high holidays. And her children are brought up in Judaism. They all went to Jewish schools. And they are lovely kids. I love them all.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experience affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it taught me not to take everything for granted. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It taught me a lot of things that, when you grow up, when you are with your parents, you should thank God for every day that you have them because you never know what you have until you lose it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's where-- see, I would like to leave a message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you leave the message, could I ask you, why did - to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted to take the story-- I wanted to tell the story for the generations to come that's going to grow up, and maybe some day watch the tape. They're never going to know me. But if I wouldn't tell the story, it would, even my children and grandchildren in time, would forget about it. They probably would remember me, but they would never know what happened to me. It's a matter of fact that when my great-granddaughter was born, I was there. And my son made a-- my granddaughter's husband made a video. I was holding my newborn great-granddaughter. And I said that I never believed it, what I went through, that I'm going to live to see three generations from me-- my son, my grandchildren, and his granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I survived probably because I had the will to live, and I wanted to prove that if none of us would survive, if no Jews would survive, it means our nation would disappear. This way, each one of us that survived is bringing new generation to this world. And that was my goal, and I think partially I achieved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience affect in any way your belief about God?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it did not. Sometimes, I have doubts. Because I used to think, what is God? What happened to our people? And then again, when there were situations like the time that my son got well without medicine or with anything, then I thought, this must be a higher power. So I had my doubts, and I had not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what message would you like to leave for your children and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, my children, my grandchildren, should never take anything for granted. And so is-- all the generations, all over the world, should never take anything for granted. And I hope that never, never again, what happened the Holocaust, not only to the Jewish race, to any race. And also, I like to leave a message to the American people, to the American-born generations, from now on each generation, that they should know that they live in the best country in the world, and not to take everything for granted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Hudes, thank you very, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome. I wish it could be a happier occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Hudes, could you please introduce your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband, Eddie Hudes, to whom I'm married for 35 years. Mr.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hudes, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Would--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your wife, what it's been like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met her, like I said, 35 years ago through my friend. My friend called me up. He used to live in Los Angeles. He called me up. And he said I want you to come over. I want you to meet a--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want you to meet a lady. And she's a very nice lady. She lost her husband [INAUDIBLE]. And I came over. It was about 35 years ago. I came over with my [INAUDIBLE], because I had no-- at this time, I had no car. And I met her. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] by the way, I thought, she not going to marry me anyways. So I like a joke I made, because after all, I lived in a farm and three children. I had three small children. And so I told my friend. OK, I met her. But will she-- and, uh-- I think after awhile he introduced me, she left outside. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your friend, you fell in love with her?

words.text: you fell in love with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My friend-- I can't say I fell in love. She-- I liked her right away, no, but-- I liked her. Then my friend told me go-- go outside and try to make a date with her. So I told my friend, I don't think so this girl will because I live-- she's a city girl, and I'm a country boy. I live in the country with three children on a farm. And she's used to-- But he said try. I went outside and try. She accepted. She accept right away the date. And she said, OK, we go meet. I ask her if I can call you again. She said yes. I called her up. And we made a date. I came over to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long was it before you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months. And six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would you like to tell to her personally, to say to her now? What would you like to tell her? [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: She's a wonderful woman. She's a wonderful-- she's a wonderful cook. She's a type that I-- I like.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it then there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: The best thing is I live with her for 35 years. And we're still OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to add something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I met my husband, at that time, of course, I told him he could call me. We saw each other a few times. And I told him not to take me seriously, because I don't intend to get married. But one day, I was curious where he lives. So I went out-- he lived in Fontana, California. Had a small ranch. So Sunday morning, I took my car, and took a ride out to Fontana. And I saw the situation he was in with three small children. It overwhelmed me, the children, being that I was a mother-- with no mother. It was only-- and when I came back, I told my aunt, which since then is deceased, this is the man I'm going to marry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now you've been married for 35--

speaker: interviewee
text: We are married. This is the 36th year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have our ups and downs like every marriage. But we made it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, the top row at the left is-- that's me, Sala Hudes. In the middle is my father, Idel Ajzenberg. To the right, is my sister, Hana-- Hana Kleinberg. My father-- Idel Kleinberg, sorry. At the bottom, is my father's mother, my grandmother, Malka Kleinberg. In the middle is my sister, Regina Kleinberg. In the very last is my mother, Basia Kleinberg. And the picture was taken approximately in 1936, Warsaw, Poland. To the right is my mother's mother, my-- my grandmother, Jenta Ajzenberg. To the right is my mother's brother, Motel Ajzenberg. I don't know when the picture was taken and where it-- it was taken in Poland, but I have no idea what year. On the very top is my late husband, Sam Ajzenberg. Second row on the left, it's me, at that time, Sala Ajzenberg. Next to me is my sister, Regina Kleinberg. On the bottom row at left is my son Aron Eisenberg. On the right is my son, Jacob Eisenberg. The picture was taken in 1946 in [NON-ENGLISH] at that time, was Poland occupied by the Russians. This picture was taken in [NON-ENGLISH] Vienna, in 1946-- 1949, on-- when we were repatriated from the camp, displaced persons camp going to Canada. This is, on my right, is my son Jacob, Jacob Eisenberg. In the middle, it's me, Sala Hudes. And on my left, pardon me, on my left is Jacob Eisenberg. In the middle is me, Sala Hudes. In the right is my son, Aron Eisenberg. The picture was taken in 1991 in Woodland Hills, California. At the left is my stepson, Mark Hudes. Next to him is my stepson, Erwin-- is my husband, Eddie Hudes. Next to him is my stepson, Jack Hudes. And at the very right is my stepson, Erwin Hudes. The picture was taken in 1993 in New York. That was taken in 1980, Montreal, Canada. On the left is my granddaughter, Liana Eisenberg. Next to her is my granddaughter, Debbie Eisenberg. Then it's me, Sala Hudes. And is next to-- holding, I'm holding my grandson, Sam Eisenberg. Next to Sam is my granddaughter, Rachelle Eisenberg. Next to her is my granddaughter, Darlene Eisenberg. The picture was taken in 1980, Montreal, Canada.
